hook: You already know how to find missing lengths using the Pythagorean theorem and properties of triangles. Now imagine two chords crossing inside a circle, or a secant and tangent drawn from a point outside a circle — the triangles formed here have special proportional relationships that go far beyond simple triangle rules. How would you find the length of a chord segment if all you know is where it intersects another chord?
concept: When two chords, secants, or tangents intersect in relation to a circle, the segments they create are always connected by a specific product rule. In plain English: the pieces of intersecting segments multiply in predictable ways depending on where the intersection happens. Formally, if two chords intersect inside a circle at point $P$, then $a \cdot b = c \cdot d$, where $a, b$ are the two segments of one chord and $c, d$ are the two segments of the other.
anatomy: Every segment relationship problem has three key components to identify: the intersection point (which determines which theorem to use — inside the circle for the Chord-Chord case, on the circle for tangent-secant, or outside for Secant-Secant or Secant-Tangent), the two segments of each chord or secant (the near and far segments from the external point), and the product equation itself. In the Chord-Chord theorem the equation is $a \cdot b = c \cdot d$. In the Secant-Secant theorem from an external point $P$, it is $\text{(whole secant 1)} \cdot \text{(external part 1)} = \text{(whole secant 2)} \cdot \text{(external part 2)}$, written as $PA \cdot PB = PC \cdot PD$. In the Secant-Tangent theorem, the tangent segment $t$ satisfies $t^2 = \text{(whole secant)} \cdot \text{(external part)}$.

worked_example.expression_latex: \text{Two chords intersect inside a circle. One chord is split into segments of length } 4 \text{ and } 9. \text{ The other chord has one segment of length } 6. \text{ Find the missing segment } x.
worked_example.description_latex: First, identify that the intersection point is inside the circle, which tells us to use the Chord-Chord theorem. The two chords cross at an interior point, so the product of each chord's two pieces must be equal — this is our starting equation.

We set up the product equation by pairing the two segments of each chord: the first chord contributes $4$ and $9$, and the second chord contributes $6$ and $x$. Multiplying each pair and setting them equal is the direct application of the theorem.
The left side simplifies to $36$. Now we have a simple one-step equation to solve for $x$.

Dividing both sides by $6$ gives $x = 6$. Always pause to check: does this make geometric sense? Since both chords would be length $13$ and $12$ respectively, and the circle is large enough to contain them, this answer is reasonable.

partial_example.expression_latex: \text{From an external point } P, \text{ a secant passes through the circle with external segment } 3 \text{ and whole length } 12. \text{ A tangent from } P \text{ has length } t. \text{ Find } t.
partial_example.description_latex: Start by identifying the configuration: we have a point outside the circle with one secant and one tangent drawn from it. This means we use the Secant-Tangent theorem, where $t^2$ equals the whole secant length times its external part.

We set up the equation using the Secant-Tangent theorem: $t^2 = (\text{whole secant}) \cdot (\text{external segment}) = 12 \cdot 3$. Notice that the external segment is the piece of the secant that lies outside the circle, which is $3$.
Simplify the right side and then take the square root to find $t$. What is the value of $t$?

practice_problems.prompt_latex: \text{Two chords intersect inside a circle. The segments of the first chord are } 5 \text{ and } 8. \text{ One segment of the second chord is } 10. \text{ Find the other segment } x.
\text{Two chords intersect inside a circle. The segments of one chord are } 6 \text{ and } x, \text{ and the segments of the other chord are } 4 \text{ and } 9. \text{ Find } x.

\text{From an external point, two secants are drawn. The first secant has an external segment of } 4 \text{ and a whole length of } 16. \text{ The second secant has an external segment of } 8. \text{ Find the whole length of the second secant.}

\text{From an external point } P, \text{ a tangent segment has length } t \text{ and a secant has external segment } 4 \text{ and whole length } 9. \text{ Given that the tangent also equals the radius of the circle and the center is } 13 \text{ units from } P, \text{ use both the Secant-Tangent theorem and the Pythagorean theorem to verify the radius.}

\text{A footbridge design uses a circular arch. A horizontal beam (chord) is divided by a vertical support into segments of } 7 \text{ ft and } 7 \text{ ft. A second chord (the vertical diameter extended) is split by the same intersection point into a segment of } 3.5 \text{ ft and unknown segment } x. \text{ Find } x.

common_mistakes: Students often add the two pairs of segments instead of multiplying them, writing $a + b = c + d$ rather than $a \cdot b = c \cdot d$. Remember the theorem is about products, not sums — always multiply the two pieces of each chord together.

When using the Secant-Secant theorem from an external point, students frequently multiply the two external segments together instead of pairing each external segment with its own whole secant length. The correct setup is $PA \cdot PB = PC \cdot PD$, where $PA$ and $PC$ are the whole secant lengths and $PB$, $PD$ are their respective external parts.

In Secant-Tangent problems, students forget to square the tangent length and instead write $t = (\text{whole secant}) \cdot (\text{external part})$. The tangent segment must be squared because it serves as both 'segments' of a degenerate chord that touches the circle at exactly one point, giving $t^2 = (\text{whole secant}) \cdot (\text{external part})$.
untested_variants: Two secants drawn from an external point where both whole-secant lengths are unknown and students must set up a system or use a given sum to solve.

Problems where the intersection point is on the circle itself (the degenerate chord case), which requires recognizing that one segment has length zero and the theorem collapses to a different relationship.
Problems combining segment relationships with arc length or sector area, requiring students to first find a chord length and then use it to compute an arc or area.
Three-dimensional contexts such as a sphere cross-section, where the same chord-chord relationship applies but students must first identify the correct cross-sectional circle and relevant chords.
